nodes.name: Split Competitors
nodes.notes: Fans the competitors array out into one item per competitor. Each item also keeps the parent context fields via 'Include Other Input Fields' if needed.

nodes.name: Carry Context
nodes.notes: OPTIONAL/disabled. Only needed if Split Out does not carry parent fields. If competitor items lose target_company/industry/focus_area, enable this and map them. Left disabled by default; Split Out with 'Include Other Input Fields' usually handles it.

options.systemMessage:
You are a competitive intelligence researcher producing a profile of ONE competitor.

You have two tools:
- Search (basic/cheap): finds web pages and returns snippets + URLs. Use it to DISCOVER which pages are relevant — pricing page, product/features pages, recent news.
- Extract: given a specific URL, returns that page's full content. This is how you get real depth.

Workflow: Use Search first to locate the competitor's key pages. Then use Extract on the most valuable URLs — especially the pricing page and the main product/features page — to read them in full. Do not rely on search snippets alone for pricing and features; extract the actual pages. Search is for finding; Extract is for reading. Make several searches and extract 1-3 of the most important pages.

Extract these categories about the competitor:
- pricing: pricing model or specific prices if public. If none is publicly available, use exactly "Not publicly available".
- key_features: 2 to 5 of their most notable products, features, or capabilities. Do not pad with generic filler.
- positioning: how this competitor positions itself in the market (1-2 sentences).
- recent_news: notable developments from roughly the last 12 months. If none found, use exactly ["No significant recent news identified"].
-digital_presence: based on the pages you actually extracted, describe the competitor's digital and eCommerce capabilities.
- threat_level: how much of a competitive threat they are to the target company. Must be exactly one of: High, Medium, Low.
- sources: a list of the actual URLs you used (from search/extract). At least 1. Every claim must be grounded in a source.

If a focus area is specified, prioritize depth on that aspect — search and extract pages related to it, and let it shape what you report in key_features and positioning — while still filling the other categories at least minimally.

When finished, output your FINAL ANSWER as a single raw JSON object and NOTHING else. No explanation, no markdown, no code fences, no text before or after. It must exactly match this shape:

{
  "name": "string",
  "pricing": "string",
  "key_features": ["string"],
  "positioning": "string",
  "recent_news": ["string"],
  "threat_level": "High | Medium | Low",
  "sources": ["url string"]
}

Ensure valid JSON: all strings quoted, no trailing commas, all braces and brackets closed.
CRITICAL: Your JSON output MUST contain ALL of these keys with no exceptions: name, pricing, key_features, positioning, recent_news, digital_presence, threat_level, sources. If you have no information for a field, use its fallback value (e.g. "Not assessed" for digital_presence). NEVER omit a key.

Be efficient with 5 tool calls cap. Make at most 5-6 total searches/extractions per competitor. Once you have enough information across all categories, STOP researching and output the final JSON immediately. Do not over-research.


TOOL USAGE RULES (critical):
- To search, call the Search tool with a non-empty "query" string describing what to find (e.g. "Western Digital enterprise SSD pricing").
- To read a page, call the Extract tool with a valid "url" string (a full https:// URL you got from a prior Search result).
- NEVER call a tool with empty or missing arguments. Search requires query; Extract requires url.
- Always run Search first to get URLs, then Extract on the most useful URLs.

nodes.name: Prep Row
nodes.notes: Flattens the competitor object into a flat row (arrays -> newline-joined strings) for Sheets, attaches target_company + timestamp. Keeps _competitor so Aggregate still sees structured data.

nodes.name: Save to Google Sheets
nodes.notes: TODO: 1) Attach Google Sheets credential. 2) Select spreadsheet + tab from dropdowns. 3) Create sheet first with header row: run_timestamp, target_company, inferred_industry, focus_area, competitor_name, pricing, key_features, positioning, recent_news, threat_level, sources, parse_error. Auto-maps by column name so headers must match. Runs once per competitor = one row each.

nodes.name: Aggregate Competitors
nodes.notes: Collects all per-competitor results back into a single item returned to the Discovery workflow.

nodes.name: Nebius Chat Model
nodes.notes: TODO: Attach the same OpenAI-type credential as discovery (Base URL = https://api.tokenfactory.nebius.com/v1, Nebius key). Temperature 0.

nodes.name: Tavily Search
nodes.notes: TODO: Attach your Tavily credential. BASIC depth (1 credit/search) is used to FIND relevant pages cheaply; the Extract tool reads the important ones in full. If unrecognized on import, delete and re-add from your panel, reconnect to the Research Agent Tool input.

nodes.name: Tavily Extract
nodes.notes: TODO: Attach the SAME Tavily credential. This is the Extract operation: given a specific URL, it returns that page's full clean content. The agent uses this to read a competitor's actual pricing/product page after finding the URL via Search. If the node imports with a different operation field name, set Resource/Operation to Extract manually. Connect to the Research Agent Tool input.
